exerciseHint.after: in the code. On save, the result on the right updates automatically (hot reload).

standalone.title: Standalone architecture
standalone.intro: Using this very app's configuration, it explains how a modern Angular app boots with standalone components and no NgModules. Four diagrams, each with reference steps and the real code.
standalone.theory:
Standalone components are Angular's modern architecture (the default since v17). Each component declares its dependencies directly in `imports` inside the `@Component` decorator, without needing an NgModule to register it.
The app boots with `bootstrapApplication()` instead of an `AppModule`. Global services are configured in `app.config.ts` with `provide*` functions that feed the root injector.
The result: every piece is self-contained, portable, and understandable in isolation.
standalone.simile: A standalone component is like a fully equipped hotel room: it comes with its own TV, bathroom, and minibar. NgModules were the whole building you had to check into at reception to access any amenity; with standalone, each room fends for itself.
examples: Create a new app without AppModule using `ng new` (standalone by default since Angular 17).
Progressively migrate a legacy NgModule app to standalone components.
Publish a component library without requiring the consumer app to import any module.

p1.title: Booting up without NgModules
p1.lead: This app has no `AppModule`. It boots directly from a root component plus a configuration object. This is the full chain, from the HTML tag to the running UI.
steps: Node 1 — `index.html`: the only anchor point. It contains just `<app-root>`; Angular replaces it with the root component on startup.
Node 2 — `main.ts`: calls `bootstrapApplication(App, appConfig)`. No `AppModule` or `platformBrowserDynamic`; the app boots straight from a component.
Node 3 — `App` (standalone): declares in its `imports` everything its template uses (`Sidebar`, `Topbar`, `RouterOutlet`). No NgModule registers it.
Node 4 — `appConfig`: an `ApplicationConfig` object with the `providers` array. This is where global services live (Router, HttpClient, ngx-translate).
Node 5 — UI ready: the app works without a single NgModule. Every piece declares its own dependencies and nothing else.

p2.title: Anatomy of a standalone component
p2.lead: Every component in this app is standalone: self-sufficient. The key is the decorator's `imports` array, which replaces an NgModule's `declarations`.
p2.cap: The root component `App` (app.ts), fully declared in a single place
steps: Field `@Component`: marks the class as a component and bundles its configuration. No NgModule declares it; the component registers itself (`standalone: true` is the default since Angular 17).

Field `imports: [...]`: the heart of standalone. Declares the components, directives, and pipes the template uses — the direct equivalent of an NgModule's `declarations` + `imports`, but per component.
Field `changeDetection: OnPush`: Angular only re-evaluates this component when its inputs change or a Signal it reads changes. The ideal pairing with the Signals API.
Fields `templateUrl / styleUrl`: separate the view and styles into their own files. The component is self-contained: everything it needs travels with it.

p3.title: Global services with provide* (ApplicationConfig)
p3.lead: Without NgModules, global services are declared in `app.config.ts`: an `ApplicationConfig` whose `providers` array uses `provide*` functions. That feeds the app's root injector.
steps: Node 1 — `appConfig.providers`: the array that replaces `NgModule.providers`. Each `provide*` function registers a global service with its configuration.

Nodes 2–4 — `provide*` functions: `provideRouter` wires up routing, `provideHttpClient` enables HTTP requests, and `provideTranslateService` sets up i18n. Each encapsulates its service's configuration.
Node 5 — Root injector: the providers feed the root injector. From here, any component can request a service without anyone declaring it in a module.
Node 6 — `inject(Service)`: the modern way to request a service from any injection context (class field, constructor, factory function). No constructor injection or `@Inject` needed.

p4.title: Lazy-loaded routes (app.routes.ts)
p4.lead: Routing is declared in `app.routes.ts`: a `Routes` array where each section is lazy-loaded. It is also a live example of Block 05 (Routing).
p4.d2: Matches the first route in the array
steps: Node 1 — URL: the user navigates to a route. The Router compares the URL against the `Routes` array in order and picks the first match.
Node 2 — Router: runs the `loadComponent` of the matched route. It is a dynamic `import()`: the component's chunk is not yet in memory.
Node 3 — `loadComponent`: downloads the chunk only when that route is visited for the first time. On subsequent visits the chunk is already in the browser's cache.
Node 4 — `<router-outlet>`: the loaded component renders here. It acts as a placeholder in the layout; its content changes with each navigation but the layout persists.

if.title: @if
if.theory:
`@if` is Angular's control-flow block for rendering content conditionally right in the template, replacing the old `*ngIf` directive.
 It supports `@else if` and `@else` to chain conditions, and the `@if (expr; as variable)` syntax to capture the evaluated value in a typed, non-null alias.
 Being native compiler syntax (not a directive), it is faster, needs no import, and hidden content is never even created in the DOM.
if.simile: Think of `@if` as a venue's bouncer: it checks a condition at the door and only lets in the content that meets it. `@else if` are its extra rules ("if you don't meet this, see whether you meet that") and `@else` is the default exit for everything that fits no rule.
examples: Show a spinner while data loads and the content once it arrives.
Render a block only if the user has a certain role or permission.
Display an "empty list" message when there are no items to show.

for.title: @for
for.theory:
`@for` repeats a chunk of template for each item in a collection, replacing the old `*ngFor` directive.
 Declaring `track` is mandatory: the expression that uniquely identifies each item so Angular knows which ones to reuse, move, or destroy when the list changes (key for performance).
 It offers contextual variables (`$index`, `$count`, `$first`, `$last`, `$even`, `$odd`) and an `@empty` block shown when the collection is empty.
for.simile: Imagine a stamp press: `@for` stamps the same template once per item in the list. The `track` is each copy's serial number: thanks to it, when the list changes the press knows which stamps to keep and which to redo instead of reprinting everything from scratch.
examples: Render a grid of products or cards from an array.
Generate a table's rows from API data.
Combine it with `@empty` to show a "no results" state.

switch.title: @switch
switch.theory:
`@switch` picks which block to render by comparing a value against several `@case`, replacing the old `[ngSwitch]`.
 The comparison is strict equality (`===`) and only the first matching `@case` is shown; there is no "fallthrough", so you don't need `break`.
 The `@default` block (optional) renders when no `@case` matches. It is ideal when a single variable decides between several mutually exclusive views.
switch.simile: Think of `@switch` as an old TV's channel dial: you turn the dial (the value) and only the channel matching that position shows; the rest stay off. `@default` is the "no signal" screen that appears when the dial points to no known channel.
examples: Show a different view depending on the user's role (admin, editor, guest).
Render an icon and color based on an operation's status (success, warning, error).
Swap the panel's content based on the selected tab.

signal.title: Signal
signal.theory:
A Signal is a value container that notifies its consumers whenever the value changes. It is created with `signal(initialValue)`.
 You READ it by calling it as a function (`count()`) and WRITE to it with `.set(value)` (replace) or `.update(fn)` (compute from the current value).
 Angular automatically tracks which parts of the template read each Signal, so only what depends on it is re-evaluated: fine-grained change detection without `Zone.js` or `ngOnChanges`.
signal.simile: A Signal is like a scoreboard: it holds a number that everyone in the stadium watches. When the referee changes it (`.set()` puts a new score; `.update()` is "+1 to the current"), every eye updates at once without anyone having to ask "what's the score?".
examples: Track the state of a counter or a venue's occupancy.
Store the current value of a form field.
Control a visibility flag (open/closed, shown/hidden).

input.title: Input
input.theory:
An Input is the channel through which a component receives data from its parent. The modern API is the `input()` function, which returns a read-only Signal.
 Declared with `input(defaultValue)` (optional) or `input.required<T>()` (required), and read in the template as a function call: `name()`.
 Being a Signal, when the parent changes the bound value the input updates and only what uses it is re-evaluated. It supports aliases and transform functions.
input.simile: An Input is like the slot of a vending machine: the child component exposes a labelled slot (`name`, `role`…) and the parent feeds the right data into it. The child can only read what it receives through the slot; it cannot reach inside and change it.
examples: Pass a user object to a profile card.
Configure a reusable button (text, variant, disabled state).
Send a list of items to a table or list component.

output.title: Output
output.theory:
An Output is the channel through which a component notifies its parent that something happened. The modern API is the `output()` function, which returns an emitter with `.emit(value)`.
 The parent listens with event-binding syntax `(eventName)="handler($event)"`, where `$event` is the emitted value.
 Together with Input it forms the one-way data flow: data flows DOWN to the child via input and notifications go UP to the parent via output. The child never mutates the parent's state directly.
output.simile: An Output is like a shop doorbell: the customer (child) rings it to signal they need something, but the shop assistant (parent) decides what to do. The bell only says "this happened"; it doesn't walk into the back room and change anything on its own.
examples: Notify the parent that "delete" was clicked on a list row.
Emit the selected value from a rating or picker component.
Signal that a child form has been submitted with its data.

computed.title: Computed
computed.theory:
A Computed is a read-only Signal whose value is derived from other signals. It is created with `computed(() => ...)`.
 Angular automatically detects which signals you read inside the function and registers them as dependencies: the computed is recalculated only when one of them changes.
 It also memoizes: if dependencies haven't changed it returns the last computed value without re-running the function. That makes it the ideal place for derived logic, instead of duplicating calculations in the template.
computed.simile: A Computed is like a spreadsheet cell with a formula (=A1*B1): you don't write its result by hand, you write what it depends on. When you change A1 or B1 the cell updates itself; if you touch nothing, it keeps its value without recalculating.
examples: Calculate a cart total from price and quantity.
Derive the number of pending tasks from a task list.
Compose a full name from first name and last name.

effect.title: Effect
effect.theory:
An Effect runs a function every time one of the signals it reads inside changes. It is created with `effect(() => ...)`, typically in the constructor or as a class field, because it needs an injection context.
 Unlike `computed()`, it returns no value: its purpose is side effects (logging, syncing with localStorage or the DOM, analytics…). These are its main characteristics:

theoryList.title: First run
theoryList.text: It runs once when the component initializes. Even if you declare it in the constructor, it does not run there: Angular runs it during the first change detection, after the component's view is rendered.

theoryList.title: Re-runs
theoryList.text: From then on it runs again on every change to any of the signals it reads inside.

theoryList.title: Automatic dependencies
theoryList.text: You don't declare what it depends on: Angular tracks the signals you read during the run. If an `if` branch never reads a signal, that signal is not a dependency on that pass.

theoryList.title: Returns no value
theoryList.text: Its output is what it triggers outside the reactive world, not a value you can read from the template.

theoryList.title: Batched and async
theoryList.text: It does not fire synchronously after each `set()`. Several changes in a row are batched into a single later run.

theoryList.title: Automatic cleanup
theoryList.text: It is destroyed on its own when the component whose injection context created it dies. If you create it elsewhere, stop it manually with the `EffectRef.destroy()` it returns.

theoryList.title: onCleanup
theoryList.text: The function receives an `onCleanup` to cancel the previous run's work (a `setTimeout`, a request) before the next one starts.

theoryList.title: untracked
theoryList.text: Wrap a read in `untracked(...)` to check a signal without making it a dependency, avoiding re-runs you don't want.

theoryList.title: Rule of thumb
theoryList.text: Use `computed` to DERIVE data; reserve `effect` for triggering things outside the reactive world.

effect.simile: An Effect is like a streetlight's light sensor: it doesn't calculate or store anything for you, it simply watches a condition (darkness falls) and triggers a real-world action (turn on the light) every time that condition changes.
examples: Save a user preference to localStorage whenever it changes.
Log/track analytics every time an important state changes.
Sync a signal with something external: document title, focus, a non-reactive library.

viewchild.title: ViewChild
viewchild.theory:
ViewChild gives you access from the class to an element or component in the template. The modern API is the `viewChild()` function, which returns a Signal holding the reference.
 It locates the target by template variable (`#field`) or component type, and is read as a function call: `field()`. Until the view renders it is `undefined`; use `?.` or `viewChild.required()` if it always exists.
 It is the tool for imperative actions that have no declarative equivalent (setting focus, measuring the DOM, integrating an external library). Use it as a last resort, not to read data you could already bind.
viewchild.simile: ViewChild is like having someone's seat number in a theatre: instead of shouting at the stalls, you walk straight to that row and seat to talk to that specific person. The `#field` variable is the seat number you put on the element.
examples: Focus a field when a form or modal opens.
Measure or scroll a DOM element.
Call a public method of a child component (a player, a map).

to-signal.title: toSignal
to-signal.theory:
`toSignal()` converts an Observable into a Signal. It is the official bridge between RxJS and Angular's modern reactive API, available from `@angular/core/rxjs-interop`.
 It subscribes to the Observable internally and tears down the subscription when the component is destroyed — no `takeUntilDestroyed` or manual `unsubscribe()`. The Signal's value updates with every new emission.
 The `initialValue` option defines what the Signal returns before the first emission, avoiding the `T | undefined` type and letting you use the Signal in `@for` from the start without extra guards.
 In modern Angular, `HttpClient` + `toSignal` replaces the Observable + `async` pipe pattern.
to-signal.simile: Imagine receiving a printed newspaper every morning (Observable): you wait for delivery and manage the subscription yourself. `toSignal` is subscribing to the digital edition: the content arrives on your device automatically, always up to date, and when you cancel the subscription (destroy the component) it stops arriving on its own — no need to call anyone.
examples: Convert an `HttpClient.get()` call into a Signal to read it directly in the template without the `async` pipe.
Derive a loading-state Signal (`loading`) from the same Observable with `initialValue: true`.
Integrate a legacy service Observable into a new component already using the Signals API.

events.title: Event Listeners
events.theory:
Event binding connects a DOM event to a component method using parentheses: `(click)="send()"`, `(input)="onInput($event)"`.
 `$event` is the native event object; for inputs you usually read `($event.target as HTMLInputElement).value`. Angular re-evaluates the view after the handler, so updating a signal is reflected immediately.
 For keyboard events there are pseudo-events like `(keydown.enter)` or `(keydown.escape)`, which avoid comparing `event.key` by hand. This is the declarative way to respond to user interaction.
events.simile: An event listener is like your doorbell wired to a specific action: when someone presses it (the event), it rings and you go to open (the handler). You decide which bell to listen to — the front door, the oven — and what to do when each one rings.
examples: Update a signal live as the user types in a field.
Submit a form when Enter is pressed with `(keydown.enter)`.
Trigger an action on button click with `(click)`.

define.title: Define routes
define.theory:
Routes are defined in a `Routes` array: each entry maps a `path` (URL segment) to the component to display, via `component` or `loadComponent` (lazy loading).
 The matched component renders inside a `<router-outlet>`. Routes are registered with `provideRouter(routes)` in the app config.
 Special paths: `''` for the empty route (often with `redirectTo`) and `'**'` as a wildcard for unknown URLs. Order matters: the router picks the first match.
define.simile: The routes array is like a building directory at the entrance: it maps each office name (the URL) to the floor and door where it is (the component). If an office isn't in the directory, no one can reach it even if the room exists.
examples: Map `/products` to the catalogue component.
Redirect the empty route `''` to a default home section.
Catch unknown URLs with `'**'` for a 404 page.

loading.title: Loading strategies
loading.theory:
There are two main loading strategies. Eager: the code is included in the initial bundle and is available on startup. Lazy: it is split into a chunk and downloaded when needed.
 At the route level, lazy loading is achieved with `loadComponent` and `loadChildren` (what this app uses). Optionally, `withPreloading(PreloadAllModules)` preloads chunks in the background after startup.
 At the template level, the `@defer` block defers part of the view based on a trigger (`on interaction`, `on viewport`, `on idle`…), with `@placeholder`, `@loading`, and `@error` blocks. Less initial bundle = faster startup.
loading.simile: Eager vs lazy is like packing for a trip: daily essentials go in your carry-on (eager, always with you); things you might not need go in checked luggage and you only collect them if required (lazy). Packing less upfront means you board faster.
examples: Defer a heavy panel (chart, map) until it enters the viewport with `on viewport`.
Load an admin route only for users who access it (`loadChildren`).
Preload probable routes in the background with `PreloadAllModules`.

params.title: Routes with parameters
params.theory:
A route parameter is a dynamic URL segment declared with a colon: `product/:id`. It matches any value (`product/1`, `product/2`) and exposes it as a parameter.
 The modern way to read it is to enable `withComponentInputBinding()` in `provideRouter`: the parameter is bound by name to the component's `input()` (`id = input.required<string>()`), as a Signal, without injecting `ActivatedRoute`.
 Query params (`?order=price`) and resolved data also arrive as inputs through the same mechanism.
params.simile: A route with a parameter is like a hotel room number: `room/:number` is the template, and `room/204` takes you to that specific one. The receptionist (the Router) reads the number from the "key" (the URL) and hands you exactly that room, not another.
examples: Open a product detail with `product/:id`.
Show a user profile with `user/:username`.
Filter or sort a list with query params (`?order=price`).

children.title: Child routes
children.theory:
Child routes nest routes inside another. The parent route renders a "layout" component with its own `<router-outlet>`, and children display there based on the next URL segment.
 They are declared in the parent route's `children` (or `loadChildren`) property. The URL reflects the hierarchy: `settings/security` = parent `settings` + child `security`.
 Key advantage: the parent layout (header, tabs, navigation) PERSISTS while children change. An empty child route with `redirectTo` defines what shows by default on entry.
children.simile: Child routes are like floors in an office building: the lobby, lifts, and reception desk (the parent layout) are the same for everyone. Only the office you enter on each floor changes (the child view). You don't rebuild the whole building every time you switch offices.
examples: A settings panel with tabs: profile, security, notifications.
A dashboard with a fixed side menu and sections that change on the right.
A product sheet with sub-routes: description, reviews, questions.

navigate.title: Navigate to routes
navigate.theory:
There are two ways to navigate. Declarative: the `routerLink` attribute in a template, for links the user clicks directly.
 Imperative (programmatic): from the class, with `inject(Router)` and `router.navigate([...])` or `router.navigateByUrl('/...')`. This is the option when navigation is a CONSEQUENCE of an action: a successful login, saving a form, a validation.
 `navigate` accepts options like `relativeTo` (resolve relative to the current route), `queryParams`, or `fragment`. Internally, `routerLink` also calls the Router; they are the declarative and imperative faces of the same thing.
navigate.simile: It's the difference between a road sign and a taxi. `routerLink` is the sign: it's there and the user decides to follow it. `Router.navigate()` is calling a taxi: your code picks the destination and leaves only when a condition is met (e.g. "when you finish paying, take me to the thank-you page").
examples: Redirect to the dashboard after a successful login.
Go back to the list after saving a form.
Jump to the next step of a wizard when "Continue" is clicked.

guards.title: Guards
guards.theory:
A guard decides whether a navigation can occur. The most common is `CanActivateFn`: a function that runs before a route is activated and returns `true` (allow), `false` (block), or a `UrlTree` (redirect).
 It is attached to a route with `canActivate: [myGuard]`. Inside the guard you can use `inject()` to get services (session state, Router, etc.).
 Functional guards replace the old classes implementing `CanActivate`: they are just functions, with no decorators or boilerplate, and compose easily. Variants exist: `CanDeactivateFn`, `CanMatchFn`, `ResolveFn`.
guards.simile: A guard is a nightclub bouncer with a guest list. Before letting you into the VIP room (the route), they check whether you're on the list (the condition). If you are, you're in; if not, they either turn you away (block) or send you to the general area (redirect with a UrlTree).
examples: Protect routes that require a logged-in session (auth guard).
Restrict an admin section by user role.
Warn before leaving a form with unsaved changes (CanDeactivate).

translate.title: ngx-translate
translate.theory:
ngx-translate is a runtime internationalization library: text lives in language files (JSON) and is referenced by key, not hard-coded in the template.
 It is set up with `provideTranslateService` and a loader (`provideTranslateHttpLoader`) that loads the JSON files from a folder. In the template you use the `translate` pipe: `{{ 'demo.titulo' | translate }}`.
 `TranslateService.use(lang)` switches the language on the fly and the pipe reacts, updating every text. It supports parameters (`{{ key | translate: { name } }}`) and a fallback language (`fallbackLang`).
translate.simile: ngx-translate is like a restaurant menu for tourists: the dish is the same (the key `demo.titulo`), but the menu describes it in each customer's language. Switching language means handing over a different menu: the dish doesn't change, only how it is named.
examples: Offer the interface in several languages with a selector.
Centralize all text in JSON files, outside the code.
Insert dynamic values into a translated sentence with parameters.

setup.title: ngx-translate · Setup
setup.intro: It documents how this very app's i18n is wired: from the initial setup to switching language on the fly, step by step and with diagrams.
setup.theory:
ngx-translate does runtime internationalization: text lives in per-language JSON files and is referenced by key, not hard-coded in the template.
 It is set up with `provideTranslateService` and a loader that loads those files. In the template, the `translate` pipe resolves each key based on the active language.
 `TranslateService.use(lang)` switches the language on the fly and the pipe reacts. This very app is built this way.
setup.simile: Setting up ngx-translate is like arranging the simultaneous interpreting at a conference: you install the booths (the loader that loads the languages), hand out headsets at every seat (the `translate` pipe on each text), and add a single channel switch (the menu) that changes the language for the whole room at once.
examples: Serve the app in several languages with a selector.
Centralize all text in JSON files, outside the code.
Insert dynamic values into a translated sentence with parameters.

p1.title: Set up the service at bootstrap
p1.lead: In `app.config.ts` you register `provideHttpClient()` (the loader needs it to fetch the JSON files) and `provideTranslateService()` with its loader, the initial language (`lang`) and the fallback (`fallbackLang`). It is the single global configuration point.

p2.title: Create the language files
p2.lead: Each text is a key with one value per language, in `public/i18n/es.json` and `en.json`. To interpolate a value, leave a placeholder with double braces. The same key must exist in both files.

p3.title: Show the text in the template
p3.lead: You use the `translate` pipe with the key. To interpolate, pass it a params object whose keys match the JSON placeholders. The pipe is reactive: when the language changes, the text updates by itself.
p3.demoLabel: Live demo (switch the language in the top right):

p4.title: Switch language on the fly
p4.lead: The menu calls `LanguageService.setLanguage(lang)`, which runs `TranslateService.use(lang)`: the loader reloads that language's JSON and the pipe re-emits every text. It also persists the choice in `localStorage` and updates `<html lang>`.

p5.title: Translate from the code (.ts)
p5.lead: You inject `TranslateService` and choose based on whether you need to react to the language: `instant()` (synchronous, not reactive), `get()` (Observable, once) or `stream()` (Observable that re-emits when the language changes). Interpolation goes as the second argument.
p5.note: Trick: `instant()` alone isn't reactive. The demo recomputes because we wrap it in a `computed` that reads the active-language signal from `LanguageService`.

decimales.title: Decimal control
decimales.theory:
An attribute directive adds behavior to an existing element (an input, a button) without rendering its own template. It is applied as an attribute: `<input [appDecimales]="2">`.
 It receives data with `@Input`. If you declare it as a SETTER (`@Input() set appDecimales(v)`), the setter runs every time the bound value changes — ideal when receiving the value involves logic (validating, transforming, reacting), not just storing it.
 To react to events on the host element, use `@HostListener` (here, the `input` event), and `ElementRef` gives access to the DOM node to read or adjust its value.
decimales.simile: An attribute directive is like an accessory you plug into an appliance: the text field stays the same, but you attach a "limiter" that controls what gets through. The `@Input` setter is that accessory's dial: every time you turn it (change the decimal count), the limiter readjusts instantly.
examples: Limit the decimals or range of a currency input field.
Highlight an element on mouse hover (the classic appHighlight).
Apply a format mask (phone, IBAN) as the user types.

desubscribirse.title: Unsubscribe
desubscribirse.theory:
When you manually subscribe to an Observable with `.subscribe()`, the subscription stays active even after the component is destroyed, causing memory leaks.
The modern Angular solution is the `takeUntilDestroyed()` operator from `@angular/core/rxjs-interop`: it receives a `DestroyRef` (injected with `inject()`) and cancels the subscription automatically when the component is destroyed. No `ngOnDestroy`, no closing Subject.
Shortcut: `async` pipe and `toSignal()` manage unsubscription automatically — no manual action needed.
desubscribirse.simile: An uncancelled subscription is like leaving a tap running when you leave the house: water keeps flowing even though nobody is using it (memory leak). `takeUntilDestroyed` is the automatic shut-off that closes the tap as soon as the door shuts.
examples: Cancel a real-time price poll when leaving the view.
Stop listening to a WebSocket when the component is destroyed.
Cancel an autocomplete search when navigating to another screen.

concat-map.title: concatMap
concat-map.theory:
concatMap is the most conservative flattening operator: it converts each emission from the source Observable into an inner Observable, but waits for the previous inner Observable to complete before subscribing to the next one. The result is a strictly sequential queue: requests execute one after the other, in the same order they were emitted, with no overlap.
It is ideal when the order of operations matters or when the results of one depend on the previous one: it guarantees that two requests will never be active at the same time.
concat-map.simile: It's like a bank teller window with a single cashier: each customer waits their turn in the queue and is served completely before the next one comes in. No parallelism, just guaranteed order.
examples: Send messages in order: each message waits for the previous one to be confirmed before being sent.
Process steps in a setup wizard where each step depends on the previous one.
Sync offline changes in creation order when the connection is restored.

merge-map.title: mergeMap
merge-map.theory:
mergeMap is the most permissive flattening operator: each value emitted by the source Observable is converted into a new inner Observable, and all inner Observables run in parallel without cancelling or ignoring each other. Responses arrive in the order they complete, not in the order they were launched.
This makes it ideal when operations are independent of each other and you want to maximise concurrency: all requests fly at once and each result is processed as soon as it arrives, without waiting for the others.
merge-map.simile: It's like several chefs working in parallel on different dishes: each one starts cooking as soon as they get their order and serves it as soon as it's done, without waiting for the others to finish. The result arrives sooner, but the order of dishes on the table depends on who finishes first.
examples: Load in parallel the posts of several users selected with checkboxes.
Upload multiple files simultaneously and show the progress of each one.
Enrich a product list by making a stock request for each one.

exhaust-map.title: exhaustMap
exhaust-map.theory:
exhaustMap is also an RxJS flattening operator, but with the opposite logic to switchMap: if a new emission arrives from the source Observable while the previous inner Observable has not yet completed, that emission is silently discarded. Only once the current inner Observable finishes does exhaustMap become available again to process the next emission.
It is ideal for preventing double form submissions or actions that must not run in parallel: the first click fires the request and subsequent clicks are ignored until the first one completes.
exhaust-map.simile: It's like a lift that won't open its doors while it's moving. You can press the button as many times as you want during the journey, but the lift won't react until it reaches its destination. The first press wins; the rest are discarded.
examples: Login button: the first click sends the credentials and subsequent clicks are ignored until a response arrives.
A «Pay» button: prevents two simultaneous charges even if the user clicks multiple times.
Starting a data export: blocks new attempts while the process is already running.

switch-map.title: switchMap
switch-map.theory:
switchMap is an RxJS flattening operator: each value emitted by the source Observable is converted into a new inner Observable (the HTTP request), and if a new value arrives while the previous one hasn't completed yet, switchMap cancels the previous inner Observable and subscribes to the new one. Only the latest inner Observable is active at any given time.
It is ideal for real-time search: if the user types quickly, intermediate requests are discarded and only the last one is processed, avoiding out-of-order responses and unnecessary requests.
switch-map.simile: It's like booking a taxi through an app and changing your destination before the driver accepts: the previous request is automatically cancelled and only the new one goes ahead. No taxis pile up on the way — only the latest order matters.
examples: Autocompleting a search field: each new keystroke cancels the previous request.
Reloading a user profile when selecting a different one from a list.
Fetching the weather forecast when the user switches cities.

intro.title: Introduction
intro.intro: Dependency Injection is the heart of Angular: understand how the injector creates, shares and delivers services so your components don't have to do it themselves.
intro.theory:
Dependency Injection (DI) is the core mechanism by which Angular creates the objects your components need and delivers them automatically. There is no need to manually call `new Service()`: you describe what you need and the injector creates it and makes it available.
The system revolves around three concepts: the token (what to ask for), the provider (how to create it) and the injector (who manages it). Angular has a root injector for global services and can create child injectors at the component or route level to isolate the service's lifecycle.
The modern way to request a service is with the `inject()` function, usable in any injection context: class field, constructor, functional guard, factory...
intro.simile: Think of a restaurant. The cooks (components) don't go to the market to buy ingredients: they tell the maître d' what they need (the token) and the provisioning service (the injector) brings it. If the ingredient is already in stock (singleton), it is reused; if not, it is created on the spot. There are never two stocks of the same ingredient in the same injector.
examples: Inject `HttpClient` to make HTTP requests without manually constructing or configuring it.
Share the authenticated user across the navigation bar, sidebar and each view with a singleton `AuthService`.
Get a reusable `LoggerService` across multiple components without duplicating instances.

p1.title: Create and register the service
p1.lead: Decorating the class with `@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })` is all you need to register it in the root injector. Angular creates a single instance (singleton) and reuses it throughout the app: if ten components ask for the service, they all receive the same instance.
p1.n2: @Injectable registers the token in the root injector
p1.n4: Singleton instance available throughout the app

p2.title: Inject the service into the component
p2.lead: `inject(TaskService)` asks the injector for the service instance. Angular looks up the token in the injector tree, finds it in the root injector and returns the same instance to everyone who requests it. No `new`, no manual lifecycle management.
p2.n2: inject() requests the token from the injector
p2.n4: Same instance shared by all consumers

p3.title: The injector tree
p3.lead: Angular doesn't have a single injector: they are organized as a hierarchical tree. The root injector serves the whole app; environment injectors (lazy routes) or component injectors can override providers locally, isolating the service in that subtree without affecting the rest.
p3.n1: Root injector — global singleton, lives for the whole app
p3.n2: Environment injector — lazy route or providedIn: 'platform'
p3.n3: Component injector — providers: [] in the component decorator
p3.n4: Token resolution: climbs the tree until found
p3.n5: providedIn: 'root' → registers in the root injector
p3.n6: providers: [...] → registers only in that tree node

exercise.title: Exercise: TaskService with Signals
exercise.desc: Create a `TaskService` that stores a task list in a Signal and complete the component that injects it with `inject()` to display and add tasks.
exercise.emptyMsg: No tasks yet. Add the first one!

subject-vs-signal.title: Subject vs Signal
subject-vs-signal.theory:
An Angular service is an injectable singleton class that centralizes logic and state shared between components. With the traditional RxJS pattern, a `BehaviorSubject` emitted values and components had to subscribe with `.subscribe()`, taking responsibility for cancelling the subscription in `ngOnDestroy` to avoid memory leaks.
With Signals, the service exposes a private `signal()` shared as `asReadonly()`: components read the value directly in the template, Angular tracks the dependency and updates only what changed. No `subscribe`, no `OnDestroy`, no `async` pipe.
The result is the same — reactive communication between components — with less code, fewer responsibilities, and less risk of bugs.
subject-vs-signal.simile: Think of the service as the central whiteboard in an office. With Subject, every employee (component) has to sign up for notifications and remember to remove themselves when they leave (OnDestroy). With Signal, the whiteboard has a digital display that everyone watches directly: no one signs up, no one forgets to unsubscribe, and the information is always current.
examples: Share the authenticated user across the topbar, sidebar, and every screen.
Propagate a shopping cart's state across multiple sections of the app.
Sync the visual theme (light/dark) chosen by the user throughout the interface.

injection-token.title: InjectionToken
injection-token.intro: Three services, three microservices, one injection point. Learn how InjectionToken decouples the component from the concrete implementation so you can swap services without touching the consuming code.
injection-token.theory:
An InjectionToken is a typed key that acts as an identifier in Angular's DI system. Unlike injecting a class directly, the token is just a reference: the injector resolves which implementation to deliver at runtime based on the configured providers.
You create one with `new InjectionToken<T>('description', { factory })`. The factory defines the default implementation. To swap the implementation in a component or route, add `providers: [{ provide: TOKEN, useClass: OtherImpl }]` without touching the consumer.
This is the Dependency Inversion principle applied to Angular: the consumer depends on the abstraction (the token), not on any concrete class.
injection-token.simile: An InjectionToken is like a standard power outlet. Your appliance (the component) only knows it needs 230 V from that socket (the token). Whether the current comes from the grid, a generator, or a solar panel (the implementations) is the installer's job (the providers), not the appliance's. You can change the source without touching the device.
examples: Inject environment configuration (API_URL, FEATURE_FLAGS) without hard-coding values into the component.
Choose between multiple service implementations based on the user's role or the current environment.
Replace a dependency with a mock in tests without modifying the component under test.

p1.title: The problem: tight coupling to a concrete class
p1.lead: Without InjectionToken, the component imports and requests the concrete class directly. If tomorrow we switch from AdminApiService to VendedorApiService, the component must be edited. With three teams and three different services, the component ends up knowing all of them.
p1.problem: Swapping services forces you to edit the component

p2.title: The solution: the token as an abstraction layer
p2.lead: With InjectionToken the component only knows the token (the abstraction). Angular's injector resolves at runtime which concrete implementation to deliver based on the configured providers. The component works the same with any of the three.
p2.n2: Typed key for ApiService. The factory defines the default.
p2.benefit: Swapping services means changing providers only — the component stays unchanged

p3.title: providers and useClass: overriding the token per component
p3.lead: The power of InjectionToken lies in providers. Adding them to the @Component decorator creates a component-scoped injector that overrides the token's factory only for that component and its descendants. The rest of the app keeps using the default.
p3.n1: Declares which implementation to use in this component tree
p3.n2: Looks up the token in its scope → finds it → delivers VendedorApiService

exercise.desc: Complete the 6 TODOs: define the abstract class, the three implementations, the InjectionToken with its factory, and the component that consumes them. Switch roles in the right panel to see how the computed signal picks the correct service.

websockets.title: WebSockets (WS / WSS)
websockets.intro: Beyond request-response: learn how to open a persistent bidirectional channel between Angular and the server to receive data in real time without making new requests.
websockets.theory:
`WebSocket` is a full-duplex communication protocol over TCP. Unlike HTTP, which follows the request-response model (the client asks, the server answers, and the connection ends), WebSocket keeps a channel open indefinitely: both the client and the server can send data at any time without waiting for the other.
The protocol starts with a special HTTP handshake: the client sends `Upgrade: websocket` in the header and the server responds with `101 Switching Protocols`. From that point on, the connection is no longer HTTP but a pure, efficient WS channel.
Angular has no built-in WebSocket service. The browser's native API (`new WebSocket(url)`) is used, wrapped in a service that exposes Observables via `fromEvent()` to integrate it with the reactive system.
websockets.simile: HTTP is like sending letters: you write one, drop it in the mailbox, and wait for a reply. WebSocket is like a phone call: the line opens once, and from then on both sides can speak whenever they want without dialing again.
examples: Real-time chat between users in a shared room.
Financial dashboard showing live stock or cryptocurrency prices updating in real time.
Collaborative document editing: one user's changes appear instantly for all others.

p1.title: The Handshake — from HTTP to WebSocket
p1.lead: A WebSocket connection starts as an ordinary HTTP request. The client includes `Upgrade: websocket` in the header and the server responds with `101 Switching Protocols`. From that moment, the connection is no longer HTTP but a persistent, bidirectional WS channel. Angular performs this process automatically when `new WebSocket(url)` is instantiated — you don't need to write any headers manually.

p2.title: Connection state with Signals
p2.lead: `WebSocket.readyState` is an integer (0–3) that indicates the connection state. In Angular, we map it to a string Signal so the template can react automatically to each state change. Each WebSocket event (`open`, `close`, `error`) triggers a `status.set()` that propagates the change to every template point that reads it.

p3.title: Bidirectional messages with fromEvent()
p3.lead: To RECEIVE messages, RxJS offers `fromEvent()`: it converts any `EventTarget` — including a WebSocket — into a typed Observable. `map()` deserializes the JSON and `takeUntilDestroyed()` cancels the subscription automatically when the component is destroyed. To SEND, `ws.send()` is called directly — without an Observable or RxJS — because it is an instantaneous imperative action.

p4.title: Lifecycle and cleanup
p4.lead: `takeUntilDestroyed()` cancels the RxJS subscription when the component is destroyed, but does not close the WebSocket connection on the server. For that, `DestroyRef.onDestroy()` is used: it registers a callback that runs when Angular destroys the component and calls `ws.close(1000)`. Code 1000 is the WebSocket standard (RFC 6455) for normal closure; the server receives it and can release resources.

exercise.desc: Complete the 4 TODOs to implement a real-time WebSocket chat. The result uses a local mock that simulates the real WS protocol: watch how the server sends a welcome message on connect and echoes back every message you send.
exercise.empty: Connect to the server to start.

http.title: HTTP (REST API)
http.intro: From GET to DELETE: learn how to connect Angular to any REST backend using HttpClient the professional way — typed, reactive, and with centralized error handling.
http.theory:
`HttpClient` is Angular's official service for communicating with REST APIs. It is registered globally with `provideHttpClient(withFetch())` in `app.config.ts` and injected with `inject()` wherever needed.
It returns Observables: streams that emit the response when it arrives. These Observables can be converted to Signals with `toSignal()` to read the result directly in the template, or consumed with `.subscribe()` when you need to react to the response with additional logic.
Encapsulating HTTP calls in a dedicated service (Service/Component separation) is the professional approach: the component only calls semantic methods (`getAll`, `create`, `remove`) and knows nothing about URLs or HTTP verbs. Error handling is centralized with RxJS's `catchError` operator.
http.simile: Think of HttpClient as your company's courier service: you prepare the package (the payload), tell it where it's going (the URL and HTTP verb), and it handles the transport. If something goes wrong (the address doesn't exist, the recipient rejects the package), it hands you back an error receipt — without you needing to know how the delivery routes work.
examples: Load a product listing from an API and render it in a table.
Submit a registration form with POST and redirect the user after a successful response.
Build an admin panel with full CRUD over a REST API resource.

p1.title: Register HttpClient with withFetch()
p1.lead: `provideHttpClient(withFetch())` registers the service in the root injector. `withFetch()` tells it to use the browser's native Fetch API instead of XHR. This brings better performance, compatibility with Angular SSR, and alignment with the web standard. You only add it once in `app.config.ts`; from there, any class can inject `HttpClient` with `inject()`.
p1.n2: Registers HttpClient with native Fetch API
p1.n4: Available in any service or component

p2.title: Define the data model
p2.lead: Before making any HTTP call, define the TypeScript interface that describes the expected response. By typing the `http.get<Post[]>()` call, Angular infers the type throughout the whole flow: from the Observable to the Signal, from the Signal to the template. `PostPayload` uses `Omit<Post, 'id'>` for create/update payloads: the backend assigns the `id`, not the client.
p2.note: Omit<Post, 'id'> — the server assigns the id

p3.title: Create the service with 4 CRUD methods
p3.lead: The service encapsulates all HTTP communication. The component only calls semantic methods (`getAll`, `create`, `update`, `remove`) and receives typed Observables. If the URL or HTTP verbs change, there is a single place to edit. `catchError` intercepts any HTTP error and transforms it into an `Error` with a readable message, before it reaches the component.
p3.n3: Transforms the request into an Observable
p3.n4: Typed response — the component only sees data

p4.title: Consume in the component with Signals
p4.lead: `toSignal()` is the official bridge between Observables (HttpClient) and Signals (modern Angular). It converts the initial GET Observable into a Signal readable in the template, manages the subscription automatically, and tears it down when the component is destroyed. For mutations (POST, PUT, DELETE), `.subscribe()` is used with `next`, `error` and `complete` callbacks, manually updating state signals.
p4.n2: Signal readable directly in the template

p5.title: Error handling with catchError
p5.lead: `catchError` intercepts the error in the service before it reaches the component. It distinguishes between network errors (status 0: no connection, CORS, server down) and HTTP errors (status 404, 500…). In both cases, `throwError(() => new Error(msg))` re-emits the error so the `error` callback in `.subscribe()` inside the component can capture it and update the error signal to show a message in the UI.
p5.n2: No error: data reaches the component
p5.n3: With error: Error with readable message to the component
p5.n4: Component updates error signal → UI shows the message

exercise.desc: Complete the 4 TODOs in the component to implement the full CRUD. The live result makes real requests to JSONPlaceholder: you will see the network data, and POST/PUT/DELETE operations return real responses (though data does not persist between sessions).

sockjs-stomp.title: WebSockets (SockJS + STOMP · FXS App)
sockjs-stomp.intro: The real architecture of a push-notification app: SockJS opens the transport (with HTTP fallback if the WebSocket fails) and STOMP layers pub/sub topic semantics on top. Although the channel is bidirectional, the app only uses it server → client.
sockjs-stomp.theory:
This stack combines two libraries: `sockjs-client` and `@stomp/stompjs`. It is not a "raw" WebSocket: SockJS handles the transport and guarantees a connection even on hostile networks (if WebSocket is unavailable, it falls back to XHR streaming or long-polling). STOMP is a messaging protocol that travels on top: it provides the pub/sub topic model, the CONNECT / SUBSCRIBE frames and destination-based routing.
The backend publishes notifications on topics like `/topic/notifications/{admin | clientId | clients}` and each client subscribes according to its role. The app never calls `send`: it is effectively a push notification channel (server → client).
STOMP does not open the socket itself: we hand it a `webSocketFactory` that returns a SockJS instance. This cleanly separates the transport layer from the messaging layer.
sockjs-stomp.simile: SockJS is the road and STOMP is the courier service driving on it. The road guarantees a vehicle gets through (and if the fast lane is closed, it uses a side road); the courier decides which mailbox — which topic — each parcel goes to. You just subscribe to your mailbox and the notifications show up on their own.
examples: Admin panel that receives every new order created by any client, live.
Per-user notifications: each client only listens to events on its own topic.
Broadcast of system notices (maintenance, status changes) to all connected clients.

p1.title: The SockJS + STOMP stack
p1.lead: Instead of `new WebSocket(url)`, the app assembles two layers. SockJS negotiates the best available transport and offers a transparent HTTP fallback. STOMP speaks its frame protocol on top, providing topic pub/sub. STOMP does not open the socket: it asks SockJS via `webSocketFactory`. SockJS needs the `global` global, which does not exist in the browser, so a polyfill points it to `window`.

p2.title: States and lazy subscription
p2.lead: The socket is not opened when the app boots, but the first time someone asks to listen to a topic (lazy init). A `BehaviorSubject` exposes the state (`UNINITIALIZED → CONNECTING → CONNECTED`). The first `onMessage()` finds the `UNINITIALIZED` state and triggers `init()`; subscriptions that arrive while connecting are queued until the state becomes `CONNECTED`.

p3.title: Handshake: CIAM vs token + /ws/info + CONNECT
p3.lead: Authentication depends on the environment. With CIAM (SSO), the cookie rides along with the request and the connection is direct. Without CIAM, we wait for the token from the store and attach it as a query param, because SockJS does not allow custom `Authorization` headers. Then SockJS does `GET /ws/info`, negotiates the transport and opens the connection; finally STOMP sends its `CONNECT` frame on top.

p4.title: Topic fan-out by role
p4.lead: The topic depends on the user's role: an admin listens to `/topic/notifications/admin` (all events), a client to its own `/topic/notifications/{clientId}`, and the general broadcast goes to `/topic/notifications/clients`. On reaching `CONNECTED`, the `BehaviorSubject` emits and all queued subscriptions are flushed: one `SUBSCRIBE` frame per topic. From then on the backend publishes toward those topics.

p5.title: End-to-end push notification
p5.lead: The backend publishes an "envelope" `{ type, body }` on the topic. `jsonHandler` parses the envelope, `NotificationService` filters by `type` and parses the nested `body`; the subscribed component shows the toast and refreshes its grid. If the network drops, `reconnectDelay: 1000` retries every second with a fresh SockJS and re-creates the subscriptions. On logout, `destroyWs()` closes everything and returns to `UNINITIALIZED`.

exercise.desc: Complete the 4 TODOs to implement the push notification channel. The result uses a local MockStompClient that emulates @stomp/stompjs: pick a role, connect and watch how the socket opens on demand, filters envelopes by type (only ORDER arrives, HEARTBEAT is discarded) and refreshes the grid with each notification.
exercise.empty: Pick a role and press Connect to subscribe to the topic.
exercise.waiting: Subscribed. Waiting for notifications from the backend…

sse.title: Server-Sent Events (SSE)
sse.intro: From polling to real push: learn how to receive a continuous stream of data from the server using plain HTTP, with automatic browser reconnection and without opening a bidirectional socket.
sse.theory:
`EventSource` is the browser's native API for Server-Sent Events: a unidirectional (server → client) protocol built on standard HTTP. Unlike WebSocket, which requires its own protocol (ws://), SSE travels over ordinary HTTP/HTTPS: the client sends a GET and the server keeps the response open, sending data in plain text format whenever it wants.
The browser automatically handles reconnection: if the connection drops, it retries without the client code doing anything. Angular consumes SSE the same way as WebSocket — with RxJS's `fromEvent()` — because `EventSource` is a standard `EventTarget`.
When to choose SSE over WebSocket: when data only flows from server to client (notifications, metrics, live logs). For bidirectional communication, WebSocket remains the right choice.
sse.simile: SSE is like an FM radio: the broadcaster (server) transmits continuously and you (client) tune in and listen. You can't reply through the same channel — that would be a phone call (WebSocket) — but for receiving real-time information, radio is simpler, more efficient, and requires no special infrastructure.
examples: Server metrics dashboard (CPU, memory, temperature) updating live without polling.
Push notification feed: the backend notifies the client when there's news without the client asking.
Real-time progress log of a long server-side process (data import, build, deploy).

p1.title: Opening the connection with EventSource
p1.lead: `new EventSource(url)` opens the connection immediately: the browser sends a GET with `Accept: text/event-stream` and the server responds with HTTP 200 keeping the connection alive. From then on, the server can send data whenever it wants. There is only one initial HTTP request — very different from polling, where the client repeatedly asks.

p2.title: Integrating EventSource with Angular Signals
p2.lead: `EventSource` implements `EventTarget`, the same interface as `WebSocket`. That's why `fromEvent()` works exactly the same way: it converts stream events into a typed Observable. `onopen` and `onerror` are `EventSource`'s own callbacks (not used with `addEventListener`) and update the status Signal so the template reacts.

p3.title: SSE protocol format
p3.lead: SSE is plain text with a very simple format: each field on its own line (`field: value`), separated by a blank line that marks the end of the event. Only `data:` is required; `event:`, `id:`, and `retry:` are optional. The `id:` field is especially useful for reconnection: the browser remembers it and sends it as `Last-Event-ID` on reconnect, allowing the server to continue from where it left off.
p3.f5: blank line — marks end of event

p4.title: Automatic reconnection and cleanup
p4.lead: Automatic reconnection is SSE's main advantage over WebSocket: if the connection drops, the browser retries without any client code doing anything. `EventSource` does not emit a `close` event when closed from the client — only from the server — so when manually disconnecting, the status Signal must be updated explicitly. Cleanup uses the same pattern as WebSocket: `DestroyRef.onDestroy()` ensures the stream is closed when Angular destroys the component.

auth-token.title: HTTP Interceptors
auth-token.intro: How to use HttpInterceptorFn (Angular 15+ functional API) to automatically inject an authentication token into every HTTP request and handle 401 errors in a centralised way.
auth-token.theory:
HTTP interceptors are functions Angular executes automatically before sending each request and when receiving the response. They centralise cross-cutting concerns: adding auth headers, logging response times, transforming errors, or renewing expired tokens — without touching each individual service.
Since Angular 15, the functional API is the recommended approach: define an `HttpInterceptorFn` and register it with `withInterceptors([myInterceptor])` inside `provideHttpClient()` in `app.config.ts`. No classes, no NgModules.
auth-token.simile: An interceptor is like airport security: every passenger (HTTP request) passes through it before boarding (reaching the server) and after landing (receiving the response). The agent can wave them through, add extra luggage (auth headers), or redirect them when something is wrong.
examples: Automatically attach the session JWT token to every private API request.
Log the URL and response time of each request to the console for debugging.
Intercept 401 responses to redirect to the login page or renew the token with a refresh token.

p1.title: Without interceptor vs with interceptor
p1.lead: Without an interceptor, each service manages its own headers. With an interceptor, the logic is centralised and all requests pass through it automatically.

p2.title: Register the interceptor in app.config.ts
p2.lead: The interceptor is passed as a function in the `withInterceptors()` array. Angular chains it automatically with all registered interceptors and runs them in order.
p2.note: Interceptors run in the order they are declared in the array

p3.title: Clone the request and add the header
p3.lead: HTTP requests are immutable in Angular. To modify them you must clone them with `req.clone()` and pass the new object to the `next()` handler.

p4.title: Handle 401 errors with catchError
p4.lead: With `catchError` in the RxJS pipe, the interceptor catches error responses before they reach the service. Ideal for redirecting to login when the session expires.

exercise.desc: Complete the 3 TODOs to implement an auth interceptor with token injection and 401 handling.
exercise.idle: Press «Simulate request» to see the interceptor in action.
exercise.hint: Without a token the request goes out without Authorization. On 401, the interceptor clears the token and simulates the redirect.

resolve.title: ResolveFn
resolve.theory:
ResolveFn is a function the router executes before activating a route. It receives the route snapshot (params, query params, static data) and returns a value, a Promise or an Observable. The router waits for the Observable to complete before rendering the destination component; the resolved data is available on `route.snapshot.data['key']` without the component managing any loading state.
Since Angular 15, `ResolveFn` replaces the class-based `Resolve<T>` interface: it is a pure function with no boilerplate, compatible with `inject()` directly in the function body.
The canonical use case is pre-loading resource data (profile, article, product) before showing the detail view, ensuring the component is created with all its data already available.
resolve.simile: A ResolveFn is like the maitre d' who checks your reservation before showing you to the table: you don't enter the restaurant (the component) until they know your table (the data) is ready. If the reservation doesn't exist, they redirect you to another room before you ever sit down.
examples: Pre-load a user's profile before rendering the detail view.
Resolve article data before activating the editor route.
Fetch role permissions before displaying the admin screen.

signals-era.title: New lifecycle in the Signals era
signals-era.theory:
Before Signals, the component lifecycle was the only mechanism for reacting to changes: `ngOnChanges` detected mutations in `@Input`, `ngAfterViewInit` indicated the DOM was ready, and `ngOnDestroy` cleaned up resources.
With Signals, reactivity is declarative and automatic: `input()` IS already a Signal and notifies every change without any hook; `viewChild()` returns a Signal that updates when the element appears in the DOM; `DestroyRef` replaces `ngOnDestroy` with a more flexible callback injectable in any context.
The result is less boilerplate, fewer hooks to remember, and more predictable reactivity: the data is always consistent because the framework tracks it, not the developer.
signals-era.simile: The classic lifecycle was like living with manual alarms: you set an alarm for `ngOnInit`, another for `ngOnChanges`, another for `ngOnDestroy`... and you had to remember to cancel them all before leaving. With Signals, it is like a smart home: lights turn on and off on their own when they detect movement. No alarms to manage; the system reacts.
examples: Automatically recalculate a final price each time the discount changes, without ngOnChanges.
Log a configuration change reactively with effect(), without ngOnInit.
Cancel an internal timer when the component is destroyed with DestroyRef, without ngOnDestroy.

p1.title: ngOnChanges → input() Signal + computed() / effect()
p1.lead: `ngOnChanges` fired whenever a decorated `@Input` changed value. With `input()`, the value IS a Signal from the very start: you can derive with `computed()` or react with `effect()`. No interface to implement, no `SimpleChanges` comparison; reactivity is automatic the moment the parent changes the binding.

p2.title: ngOnInit → constructor + inject() + afterNextRender()
p2.lead: `ngOnInit` was the safe initialization place because `@Input` was not available in the constructor. With `input()` Signal and `inject()`, services and inputs are declared as class fields and are available from construction. For code that needs the real DOM, `afterNextRender()` —new in Angular 17— is more precise than `ngOnInit`: it runs exactly once after the first real render.
p2.n1: Constructor — inject() available, input() Signal ready

p3.title: ngOnDestroy → DestroyRef.onDestroy()
p3.lead: `ngOnDestroy` required implementing the `OnDestroy` interface in the class. `DestroyRef` —injected with `inject(DestroyRef)`— lets you register cleanup callbacks in any injection context: inside the class, in a utility function, in a service. No interfaces, no boilerplate. The result is identical: the callback runs when Angular destroys the component.
p3.n2: Angular runs all callbacks registered in DestroyRef

p4.title: ngAfterViewInit → viewChild() Signal + afterNextRender()
p4.lead: `ngAfterViewInit` guaranteed that template elements existed in the DOM. With `viewChild()`, the element reference is a Signal: `undefined` before the first render, the real `ElementRef` after. For code that needs the DOM (focus, external libraries, measurements), `afterNextRender()` is the direct and more explicit replacement.
p4.n2: viewChild() Signal goes from undefined → ElementRef
p4.n4: DOM available — focus, external libraries, measurements

exercise.desc: Complete the 4 TODOs so the child component reacts to price and discount changes using the Signals paradigm, without implementing any lifecycle hook.
