exercise.hint: Watch how the final price recalculates automatically with computed(), effect() logs each change, and DestroyRef cleans up without ngOnDestroy.

control-value-accessor.title: ControlValueAccessor
control-value-accessor.theory: ControlValueAccessor (CVA) is the contract that connects a FormControl with a custom UI component. By implementing its 4 methods —writeValue, registerOnChange, registerOnTouched and setDisabledState— your component binds to formControlName or ngModel exactly like a native <input>. The FormControl doesn't know (and doesn't need to know) how the control is rendered: the CVA translates in both directions, form to UI and UI to form.
control-value-accessor.simile: A CVA is like a universal plug adapter: the electrical grid (the FormControl) always delivers current the same way, but each appliance (your component) has different pins. The adapter translates in both directions —current flows into the appliance (writeValue) and the appliance signals back to the grid (onChange)— without either one having to know the other.
examples: A 3-state checkbox (true / false / null) for search filters.
A star-rating picker integrated into a reactive form.
A phone input with mask and country prefix exposing a single clean value.

exercise.legend: Click each checkbox to cycle: null (—) → true (✓) → false (empty).
exercise.hint: Filters set to null are omitted from the payload: that state means "no preference, don't filter by this field". Also try Clear (reset → writeValue) and Disable (setDisabledState).

reactive.title: Reactive Forms
reactive.theory: In a reactive form the model lives in the TypeScript class, not in the template: you create a typed FormGroup (with NonNullableFormBuilder no control ends up null after a reset) and the template only binds to it via formControlName. Since every control exposes valueChanges as an Observable, the form becomes a data stream: with toSignal you turn it into a Signal and with computed you derive state (a filtered table, a payload…) without manual subscribes. And because the model is programmatic, you can orchestrate controls against each other: disable one based on another's value or force cascading values.
reactive.simile: A reactive form is like a recording studio mixing console: every field is a channel with its own fader, but the sound engineer at the console (your TS class) is in charge, not the musicians (the template). The console can mute one channel when another kicks in (disable) or automatically adjust one fader when you move another (setValue), and the master output (toSignal + computed) mixes every channel into a single final signal.
examples: Search filters for a table or listing, with state that can sync to the URL.
Dynamic forms generated from backend configuration (FormArray).
Multi-step wizards with cross-validation and dependent fields between steps.

exercise.busquedaError: Type at least 2 characters to search.
exercise.precioMaxDisabledHint: Disabled: only free libraries are shown.
exercise.hint: Try both interactions: check "Free libraries only" and watch the max price get disabled (and vanish from form.value); change the category and watch it force the sort order to Downloads. The search box only filters from 2 characters on, with a 250 ms debounce.

custom-validators.title: Custom Validators (factory function)
custom-validators.theory: A Validator in Angular is just a function that takes an AbstractControl and returns an errors object or null. When the validator needs parameters (a limit, a list, another field to compare against) it cannot be that function directly: you wrap it in a factory function that receives the configuration and returns the ValidatorFn with those parameters captured in its closure. It is exactly the pattern of the built-in validators: Validators.minLength(2) is a factory you pass the 2 to. On top of that, the error object can carry metadata (max values, current digits…) that the template interpolates to build rich messages, and a validator can be applied at FormGroup level to validate several fields at once.
custom-validators.simile: A validator factory is like a die-cutting machine for molds: the machine doesn't validate any part by itself; you give it the measurements (the parameters) and it manufactures a specific mold (the ValidatorFn) that is then applied part by part (control by control). Validators.minLength is a die-cutter Angular ships out of the box; writing your own means manufacturing your own molds with your business's measurements.
examples: Rejecting reserved or already-taken values from a configurable list (usernames, slugs, package names).
Numeric limits driven by business rules: number of digits, ranges per user profile, max amounts per currency.
Cross-field validation at FormGroup level: password and confirmation, start date before end date.

exercise.nombreReservadoError: "{{nombre}}" is already reserved in the registry.
exercise.hint: Try all three validators: type "angular" as the name (nombreDisponible), enter more than 4 digits in downloads and notice how the message interpolates the error metadata (maxCifras), and type two different emails: that error lives in form.errors, not in any control (coinciden).

modulo-ciclico.title: Turn an array into a cyclic carousel - %
modulo-ciclico.theory:
The modulo operator (`%`) returns the remainder of a division: `7 % 5` is `2`. Its practical superpower: `index % list.length` always lands in the range `[0, length - 1]`, no matter how large the index grows.
That turns any array into a carousel: you let a counter grow forever and the modulo "folds" it so it wraps around when it reaches the end, with no ifs and no resets.
One caveat: in JavaScript the modulo of a negative number is negative (`-1 % 5 === -1`), so to step backwards you add the length before applying the modulo.
modulo-ciclico.simile: Modulo is the face of a clock: the hours keep growing (1pm, 2pm… 3am the next day), but the hand always points between 1 and 12 because completing a lap starts over. `% 12` is exactly that automatic wrap-around.
examples: Looping over a catalog of templates when adding items (as in exercise 2.2 for @for).
An image or testimonial carousel where "next" after the last item returns to the first.
Alternating styles by position (zebra rows, a color palette that repeats every N items).

clamp.title: Numeric increment cap - Math.min or Math.max
clamp.theory:
`Math.min` and `Math.max` with two arguments return the smaller or the larger of the pair. Used against a constant they become limits: `Math.min(x, MAX)` is a ceiling (never returns more than MAX) and `Math.max(x, MIN)` is a floor (never less than MIN).
Mind the naming twist: `min` sets the UPPER limit and `max` the LOWER one. The way to keep it straight is to read it as "keep the smaller/larger of the two".
Chained together, `Math.min(Math.max(x, MIN), MAX)` forms a "clamp": it fits any value inside a range without a single if.
clamp.simile: They are the stops on a sliding window: you can push it as hard as you like, but the rail has a stop at the end (Math.min) and another at the start (Math.max). The window always stays between them, no matter how hard you push.
examples: Bounded counters: room capacity, item quantity in a cart (as in exercise 3.1 for Signal).
Zoom, volume or brightness controls that move within a fixed range.
Sanitizing values coming from outside (user input, query params) before using them in the UI.

ng-container-template.title: ng-container and ng-template
ng-container-template.intro: Two tools to control what reaches the DOM and what doesn't: templates that only exist as a mold until you instantiate them, and groupers that vanish without a trace.
ng-container-template.theory:
`<ng-template>` declares a view fragment that Angular does NOT render where it is written: it stores it as a `TemplateRef` (a mold) and it only shows up on screen when something instantiates it, for example the `[ngTemplateOutlet]` directive.
`<ng-container>` is a logical grouper that produces no element in the rendered DOM: it groups content or hosts directives without adding the typical wrapper `<div>`.
Together they enable the most powerful pattern: declare a template ONCE and reuse it in several places, feeding it different data through `[ngTemplateOutletContext]`.
ng-container-template.simile: ng-template is the cookie cutter in the kitchen drawer: owning it produces no cookies; only when you press it into the dough (ngTemplateOutlet) does one come out, and with each different dough (the context) a different cookie comes out of the SAME cutter. ng-container is the baking paper: it helps you group and handle the cookies while you bake, but it is not part of the result — nobody eats the paper.
examples: Reusing the same card/row in several areas of the view (featured + list) without duplicating HTML.
Grouping table rows under an @if without breaking the structure (a <div> inside <table> is invalid HTML).
Configurable components that accept a custom template from outside (table columns, list item, select option).

p1.title: ng-template: declaring a mold that isn't painted
p1.lead: Everything you wrap in `<ng-template>` is declared but invisible: Angular compiles it into a `TemplateRef` and keeps it in memory as a mold. It doesn't enter the DOM until something instantiates it. This is the same mechanism `@if` or `@for` use internally: their contents are templates Angular stamps only when needed.
p1.n2: Angular keeps the mold in memory
p1.n3: Only enters the DOM when something stamps it

p2.title: ng-container: grouping without polluting the DOM
p2.lead: A wrapper `<div>` creates a real node, and sometimes that's harmful: it breaks grid/flex layouts designed for direct children and produces invalid HTML inside <table> or <ul>. `<ng-container>` groups content and hosts directives just the same, but vanishes at render time: the final DOM only contains its children.
p2.note: The final DOM is identical… minus the wrapper

p3.title: Instantiating the mold with ngTemplateOutlet
p3.lead: The reference variable `#miembroTpl` captures the mold's `TemplateRef`. The `[ngTemplateOutlet]` directive stamps it right where it lives. Using it on an `<ng-container>` is the perfect combo: the template renders at that spot without the host adding any extra element to the DOM.
p3.n3: The mold's content, now in the DOM

p4.title: Passing data with the context and reusing
p4.lead: `[ngTemplateOutletContext]` takes an object with the data for that instance. The special `$implicit` key is the "default" datum: any `let-whatever` without an assignment receives it. The remaining keys bind by name (`let-rol="rol"`). Here lies the power of the pattern: the SAME template stamped N times, each with different data — zero duplicated HTML.
p4.n1: Object with the data for THIS instance

exercise.desc: Complete the 4 TODOs: declare the member template with its context ($implicit + rol), instantiate it for the featured member, reuse it inside the @for with ng-container and add the empty-state template. Add and remove members to watch the SAME mold get stamped with different context.
exercise.empty: The team is empty. Add the first member to instantiate the template.

ng-content.title: Content projection with ng-content
ng-content.theory:
`<ng-content>` marks the exact spot where a component inserts the content its parent writes between its tags: this is the content projection mechanism.
With `select` you open multiple slots that filter by CSS selector — element (`select="h4"`), attribute (`select="[tarjeta-icono]"`) or class (`select=".acciones"`) — while a `<ng-content>` without `select` acts as the wildcard: it collects everything no other slot captured.
If a node doesn't match any selector, `ngProjectAs` redirects it to the slot you choose (very handy on `<ng-container>`, which adds no nodes to the DOM). And since Angular 18, whatever you write INSIDE `<ng-content>` is fallback content: it renders only when the parent projects nothing into that slot.
Key point: projected content belongs to the PARENT — its bindings, handlers and styles resolve in the parent's context; `ng-content` only places it, it never creates or destroys it.
ng-content.simile: A component with ng-content is like a photo frame with die-cut openings (passe-partout): the frame decides how many openings there are, their shape and where they go (the slots with select), but you choose which photo goes in each opening — and the photos are still yours (the content belongs to the parent). If a photo doesn't fit an opening's format, you add an adapter so it fits (ngProjectAs). And if you leave an opening empty, you see the sample picture the frame ships with (the fallback).
examples: UI container components (Card, Modal, Accordion) that provide the frame and styling while each consumer fills in header, body and footer.
Layouts with named zones: a toolbar with start/center/end slots, or a side panel with a fixed header and free-form content.
Form-field wrappers that project the label, the control and the error messages into the same accessible structure.

exercise.desc: Complete the 5 TODOs: declare the card's slots by attribute, element and class, the body catch-all and the footer fallback; then use ngProjectAs to project an ng-container into the actions slot. Click the buttons: their (click) handlers live in the PARENT even though they render inside the card.
exercise.cardABody: Builds the guide's interface. Standalone components, Signals and plenty of handcrafted CSS.
exercise.cardBBody: Maintains the API and the deployment pipelines. Fond of clean logs.
exercise.cardCBody: Automated account: it posts the result of every build. Nobody messages it (yet).

web-components.title: Angular Web Components library
web-components.intro: The real wiring set up in this workspace: the wc-lib project builds web components with Angular Elements, the wc-demo project consumes them as HTML tags, and the resulting package is published to public npm or to your company's private Artifactory. Eight diagrams, each paired with its step and the real code.
web-components.theory:
Angular Elements (@angular/elements) turns an Angular component into a standard browser custom element: an HTML tag (`<wc-user-card>`) that works on any page, with or without Angular behind it.
The deliverable is not an ng-packagr package but a self-contained JS bundle: it carries the Angular runtime inside and, when loaded with a `<script type="module">`, registers the tags via `customElements.define()`.
Each `input()` is exposed as a property and an attribute of the element, and each `output()` is dispatched as a CustomEvent. In this workspace: `projects/wc-lib` (the factory) and `projects/wc-demo` (the consumer).
web-components.simile: Publishing a web components library is like bottling a ready-to-serve recipe: everything goes inside the bottle — the ingredients (your components) and the cook (the Angular runtime). Whoever receives it doesn't need to know how to cook Angular: they open the bottle with a <script> and serve the tag on their plate, whether their kitchen is React, WordPress or plain HTML.
examples: A corporate design system shared across Angular apps, React apps and legacy portals.
Angular widgets embedded in a CMS or legacy portal (WordPress, SharePoint, JSP…).
Micro-frontends where each team ships pieces built with its own stack and version.

run.title: Start the library and the demo
run.lead: The whole setup already lives in the repo: you just need to start it. A key note about the «from where»: all three scripts are ALWAYS run from the repo root, never by cd-ing into projects/wc-lib or projects/wc-demo — the Angular CLI finds each project by its name in angular.json, not by the current directory. You get two workflows: A) develop the library in isolation, and B) see it consumed by the demo.

r0.what: Installs the workspace dependencies, including @angular/elements (the package that turns components into custom elements).
r0.why: Without node_modules no ng build/serve will start. Only needed after cloning or when dependencies change.

r1.what: Serves projects/wc-lib alone using its own index.html: a test bench with the <wc-user-card> and <wc-contador> tags without any Angular app around them. With hot reload.
r1.why: It's the comfortable way to iterate on the web components: edit the component and the browser refreshes instantly, without going through the demo.

r2.what: First runs build:wc-lib (produces dist/wc-lib/browser/main.js) and then serves projects/wc-demo, which loads that main.js as an asset and consumes the tags via property binding and CustomEvent.
r2.why: The prior build is mandatory: the demo imports no wc-lib code, it only loads the already-compiled bundle, so it must exist in dist/ before serving. Note: main.js is a static asset — if you edit the library while the demo is running, you must re-run this script.

r3.what: Compiles the library to dist/wc-lib/browser/main.js (no hash) without starting any server.
r3.why: Useful when you just want to regenerate the bundle: before packaging/publishing, or to inspect the output.

r4.what: Builds, copies the publishable package.json into dist/ and runs npm pack, producing a .tgz with exactly what would be uploaded to the registry.
r4.why: Verifies the publish flow and which files make it into the package, without actually publishing anything.

r5.what: Starts the main guide app (this very application with the learning sections).
r5.why: It's independent from wc-lib and wc-demo; included as a reminder that the three projects coexist on different ports and don't clash.

p1.title: One workspace, three projects
p1.lead: angular.json is the workspace map: it declares each project, where its code lives (root/sourceRoot) and how it is built (architect.build). The guide app owns the repo root; new projects are born under projects/ because newProjectRoot says so. Each project builds and serves independently: ng build wc-lib doesn't touch the others.

p2.title: The two paths: application vs library
p2.lead: For web components the deliverable is a JS bundle any page can load, so we generate an APPLICATION whose main.ts registers the elements. The ng generate library + ng-packagr path produces an Angular package (FESM + metadata) that only another Angular app can compile: it's the right choice to share components BETWEEN Angular apps, but useless for plain HTML, React or a CMS.

p3.title: From component to custom element
p3.lead: createCustomElement() wraps a regular Angular component in a standard HTMLElement class. The bridge is automatic: each input() becomes a property and a dash-case attribute of the element, and each output() is dispatched as a CustomEvent carrying the emitted value in event.detail. With ViewEncapsulation.ShadowDom the styles stay isolated from the host page.

p4.title: main.ts without a root component
p4.lead: There is no bootstrapApplication(App) and no <app-root>: createApplication() boots the Angular environment (injector, zoneless change detection) without rendering anything. With that injector, customElements.define() registers each tag in the browser's global registry: from that moment any <wc-user-card> in the document upgrades itself, whether it already exists or is created later.

p5.title: Build: a single main.js
p5.lead: Angular's esbuild builder, with a single entry point and no dynamic import(), emits a single ESM main.js (~114 kB raw, ~34 kB transferred). Two key settings: outputHashing "none" so the file name stays stable (consumers reference it in a fixed <script>) and styles: [] because each component carries its styles inline. Budgets are raised: the bundle embeds the Angular runtime.

p6.title: The full wiring, file by file
p6.lead: The whole chain: main.ts compiles to dist/wc-lib/browser/main.js; wc-demo's angular.json copies it as an asset to wc-lib/main.js; the demo's index.html loads it with <script type="module"> in the <head> — BEFORE the app scripts, because module scripts run in document order, so the element is already defined when Angular bootstraps —; and the template uses <wc-user-card> thanks to CUSTOM_ELEMENTS_SCHEMA. The demo doesn't import a single line of wc-lib code: it only consumes its tags.

p7.title: Option A · Public npm publish
p7.lead: The publishable package is the dist folder plus a minimal package.json: a scoped name (@my-scope/wc-lib), version, and exports pointing to main.js. With an npmjs.com account: npm login and npm publish. Mind the pitfall: scoped packages are PRIVATE by default, and private publishing on npmjs requires a paid plan; to publish publicly and for free you must pass --access public.

p8.title: Option B · Private on JFrog Artifactory
p8.lead: Artifactory hosts three npm repos: LOCAL (where you publish your packages), REMOTE (proxy and cache of the public npmjs registry) and VIRTUAL (the view that aggregates both). The project's .npmrc routes the @company scope to the virtual feed and adds the auth token (generated from "Set Me Up"). You publish to the local repo; you always install from the virtual one: a single endpoint resolves both your private packages and the public ones.

extends.title: Extending classes (extends)
extends.intro: A base class concentrates the shared logic — signals, computed, methods and inputs — and every child component inherits it with extends, contributing only what makes it unique: its template and its styles.
extends.theory:
Inheritance in Angular is TypeScript's: a component, directive or service can `extends` another class and inherits its properties and methods… and also its `input()`, `output()`, host bindings and lifecycle hooks.
What is NEVER inherited is the decorator metadata: template, styles, selector and providers are defined by each child, because they are the component's visual identity, not its logic.
The modern enabler is `inject()` in field initializers: the base class gets its dependencies without declaring a constructor, and children are freed from the `super(dep1, dep2…)` chaining that made classic inheritance painful.
extends.simile: A food-truck franchise: the parent company provides the sauce recipe, the processes and the suppliers (the base class logic, inputs and services). Each food truck inherits all of that when it signs the franchise (extends), but the sign, the decoration and the menu it shows customers (template, styles and selector) are its own choice — and if one wants to give the recipe a personal twist, it cooks on top of the parent's (override + super).
examples: Base class for list components with shared filtering, sorting or pagination.
Base directive with common behaviour (focus, keyboard, accessibility) extended by several variants.
Base data-access service (generic CRUD) that domain services extend and specialise.

p1.title: The base class: shared logic, no template
p1.lead: All the logic that would be repeated in every list is written ONCE in an abstract class: the search term (signal), the derived filtering (computed) and the configurable input(). It is marked with a selector-less `@Directive()` because it declares an input() and Angular only processes inputs/outputs of decorated classes; and `abstract` prevents instantiating it on its own while forcing every child to provide its data.

p2.title: The child: extends + its own view
p2.lead: The child declares `extends ListaFiltrableBase` and gets the input(), the signals, the computed and the methods for free: its class is almost empty. What the base cannot give it is the decorator metadata: template, styles, selector and providers are its own. That is why two children of the same base can look completely different while sharing 100% of the logic.
p2.n2: Inherits the logic, brings the view
p2.note: Logic travels with extends; visual identity never does

p3.title: Specialising: override + super
p3.lead: A child can redefine a base method. TypeScript requires marking it with `override` (this project enables "noImplicitOverride"): if the base ever renames the method, the compiler warns instead of leaving behind an orphan method that no longer overrides anything. Inside, `super.limpiar()` reuses the parent's logic and then the child adds its own — specialise without duplicating.
p3.n1: The child redefines the inherited method
p3.n2: First it reuses the base logic…

p4.title: inject() in fields: inheritance without the toll
p4.lead: With classic constructor DI, the child had to know ALL the base's dependencies and re-pass them with super(...); if the base added one, every child broke. With `inject()` in field initializers each class serves its own dependencies: the base has no constructor, the child has no super(). This change is what made base classes a comfortable pattern again in modern Angular.
p4.n1: The child re-passes the base's deps
p4.note: No constructor means no super() to maintain

exercise.desc: Complete the 4 TODOs: declare the inheritance with extends in lista-tecnologias, complete the inherited filtering computed in the base, override limpiar() in lista-frutas with override + super, and bind from the parent the placeholder input() the children inherit. Type in the search boxes: the SAME inherited logic filters two totally different views.
exercise.hint: Two components, a single piece of logic: both inherit from ListaFiltrableBase.

abstract.title: Abstract classes
abstract.intro: An abstract class is half contract, half implementation: its abstract members force every child to define its part, and its concrete methods are inherited ready-made. In Angular it also has a superpower: since it survives at runtime, it can be used as a DI token.
abstract.theory: A class marked abstract cannot be instantiated with new: it exists only to be extended. Inside, two kinds of members coexist: abstract ones, with no body, which the compiler FORCES every child to implement; and concrete ones, with a body, inherited as-is. Unlike an interface — which is erased at compile time — an abstract class compiles to real JavaScript, and that is why Angular can use it as a dependency-injection token with useClass.
abstract.simile: The official blueprint of a housing development: nobody can live in the blueprint (it cannot be instantiated), the blueprint demands that every house has a door, a roof and a chimney (abstract members: every house MUST solve them its own way), and at the same time it ships the shared plumbing and wiring already solved (concrete methods inherited ready-made). Every built house complies with the blueprint, but chooses its own finishes.
examples: Abstract base class for reusable form components (the contract forces every control to implement its validation).
Abstract class as a DI token to swap implementations: mock/real in tests, memory/localStorage/HTTP in production.
Template-method pattern in export services: CSV and PDF share the flow and each contributes only its format.

p1.title: The abstract contract: enforce without implementing
p1.lead: An abstract member declares WHAT must exist without saying HOW: it has no body, and every child is forced to implement it or TypeScript will not compile. The rest of the class is regular implementation, inherited ready-made. The compiler watches both directions: it forbids `new NotificadorBase()` (error TS2511) and forbids a child that leaves the contract unfulfilled (error TS2515).
p1.contractCap: No body: the child provides it

p2.title: Abstract class vs interface: surviving the compiler
p2.lead: An interface also defines a contract, but it is ERASED at compile time: it only exists for the type-checker and nothing of it remains in the final JavaScript. An abstract class compiles to a real function: it can carry implementation and shared state next to the contract and — the key difference in Angular — it is a value that exists at runtime, so it can be used as a DI token. Rule of thumb: interface to shape data; abstract class when there is also code to share or a token to inject.
p2.note: Only what exists at runtime can be a DI token

p3.title: Template-method pattern: the base orchestrates, the child fills in
p3.lead: A CONCRETE method of the base defines the whole flow once — validate, register, clear — and delegates exactly the variable step to an abstract method that each child implements its own way. No child repeats the flow or can skip the validation: it only fills in its slot. That is the template-method pattern: the skeleton lives in the parent, the personality in the children.
p3.n2: The abstract slot: each child fills it in
p3.n3: …and the shared flow continues in the base

p4.title: Abstract class as a DI token
p4.lead: Because the abstract class exists at runtime, it works as a key in the injection system: the provider `{ provide: HistorialBase, useClass: HistorialEnMemoria }` declares "when someone asks for the contract, deliver this implementation". Consumers call `inject(HistorialBase)` and depend only on the contract: swapping the implementation (memory → localStorage → HTTP → mock in tests) means touching ONE provider line and no consumer ever notices.
p4.n1: The consumer asks for the CONTRACT

exercise.desc: Complete the 4 TODOs: mark NotificadorBase and its contract as abstract, complete the enviar() template method in the base, implement formatear() in the SMS channel (trim to 25 characters) and configure the provider { provide: HistorialBase, useClass: HistorialEnMemoria } in the parent panel. Send messages through both channels: each child applies ITS format, but everything ends up in the SAME history — the single instance delivered by the abstract token.
exercise.hint: Two channels, one contract: both inherit from NotificadorBase and write to the same injected HistorialBase.
exercise.empty: No notifications yet: send one from any channel.

dev-remote.title: DEV proxy without running a local backend
dev-remote.intro: In development, the Angular dev-server can act as a middleman: it intercepts calls to /api and /ws, injects your already-authenticated session cookie and forwards them to a remote environment. You work locally against real data without starting any microservices or going through the login. Five steps, each with its diagram, plus a flow simulator.
dev-remote.theory:
A development proxy redirects, on the server side, certain routes of your app towards another origin. The trick: the app requests RELATIVE routes (/api instead of https://remote…/api), so for the browser everything goes to localhost and there is NO cross-origin to block.
The jump to the remote domain is made by the dev-server, where CORS does not apply. Along the way, the proxy injects the session cookie and the Origin/Referer/Host headers, so the backend believes the request comes from its own, already logged-in web app.
Result: the app runs locally against real data without starting any backend.
dev-remote.simile: It is like a hotel concierge running errands for you: you ask «bring me something from the shop across the street» (a relative route, /api) and they cross the road, walk in with YOUR already-stamped membership card (your session cookie) and come back with the real product. You never leave the hotel (localhost) nor show your ID at the door (the login): the concierge (the proxy) is the face and the credentials on your behalf.
examples: Debug an app locally against the test or staging environment without deploying or running its microservices.
Reproduce a bug that only shows up with real data, using the browser's already-authenticated session.
Develop the front end of a feature whose backend does not run locally yet but does exist in a remote environment.

p1.title: The proxy file: what to intercept and where to forward
p1.lead: At the workspace root, proxy-dev.config.js exports an array via module.exports: one object per prefix to intercept. Each carries context (the prefix, /api or /ws), target (the remote URL), changeOrigin and secure:false + agent to accept self-signed certificates. The onProxyReq hook injects the session cookie and the Origin/Referer/Host headers into every request. For WebSockets you add ws:true and duplicate the injection in onProxyReqWs (the upgrade), because SockJS makes HTTP calls before opening the socket.

p2.title: The environment: relative routes, not absolute
p2.lead: You create a specific environment (environment.proxy.ts) where all API URLs are relative routes (/api, /ws) instead of pointing to an absolute localhost. Why does it matter so much? Because a relative route goes to the dev-server itself and the browser does not treat it as cross-origin: it does not block it. Only then does the proxy have something to intercept.
p2.note: The relative route is what lets the proxy come into play

p3.title: angular.json: build + serve
p3.lead: You define the dev-proxy configuration in two places. In architect.build.configurations, a fileReplacements swaps environment.ts for environment.proxy.ts at build time. In architect.serve.configurations, an entry with buildTarget pointing at that build (project:build:dev-proxy) and proxyConfig pointing at the Step 1 file. This way serve compiles with relative routes and starts with the proxy active.

p4.title: The script and the full flow
p4.lead: You add the start:proxy script to package.json: ng serve with --configuration=dev-proxy. When you run it: Angular compiles with relative routes → the app requests /api → it goes to localhost:4200 → the dev-server sees it matches the context and intercepts it → the proxy injects your cookie and forwards to the remote → the remote responds 200 with real data. No login, no local microservices.

p5.title: Maintenance: the cookie expires
p5.lead: Session cookies are short-lived. When the proxy starts returning 401s or login redirects, the cookie has expired: log in to the remote again, copy the updated cookie from DevTools and paste it into the proxy file. For the same reason, treat this file as sensitive: it holds a live credential, so add it to .gitignore to never commit a real cookie.
p5.sensitive: ⚠ Sensitive file · do not commit real cookies

exercise.desc: The proxy renders nothing, so the exercise reproduces its DECISION LOGIC. Complete the 4 TODOs in resolverPeticion(): match the context, return 404 when there is none, 401 with an expired cookie and 200 on the happy path. Then use the simulator: type routes and toggle the cookie to see when the proxy intercepts, when it injects the session and what the remote responds.
exercise.hint: Type a route and toggle the cookie: watch the Browser → dev-server → proxy → remote flow light up.

ok200.title: 200 · Real data
ok200.text: The proxy matched the context, injected your cookie + Origin/Referer/Host and forwarded to the remote, which responded with real data. No login, no local backend.

err401.title: 401 · Expired cookie
err401.text: The proxy forwarded the request, but the remote rejected the session and redirects to login. Re-copy the cookie from DevTools and paste it into proxy-dev.config.js.

err404.title: 404 · Not intercepted
err404.text: The route does not match any context (/api, /ws). The proxy leaves it alone: it stays on the dev-server (localhost:4200), where that resource does not exist.
